ontology.foodex2_description: Broad beans (Vicia faba), processed — deep-fried, salted snack
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers processed legume products; no exact deep-fried salted broad bean code exists. Closest defensible category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for deep-fried salted broad beans identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical snack serving of deep-fried salted broad beans is approximately 30 g, equivalent to a small handful.

nutrient_source.title: Deep-fried broad bean, salted — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for deep-fried broad bean, salted (F09010005). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for deep-fried fava beans (NDB 16052) as an analogous product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for deep-fried salted broad beans found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the published GI of broad beans / fava beans (Vicia faba) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists broad beans at GI ~40. Deep-frying and salting are not expected to substantially raise GI given the high fibre (12.3 g/100g) and protein (23.8 g/100g) content. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (40 × 52.5 × 0.30) / 100 = 6.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 397 mg/100g approaching the high-sodium threshold; deep-fried preparation adds cardiovascular burden
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 30 g or less per serving (contributing ~119 mg sodium) and choose unsalted roasted broad beans as a lower-sodium alternative; the high dietary fibre (12.3 g/100g) and estimated potassium content provide partial offset but do not neutralise the sodium load at larger servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (52.5 g/100g) moderated by exceptional dietary fibre (12.3 g/100g) and high protein (23.8 g/100g); estimated low GI (~40)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume deep-fried salted broad beans in small portions (30 g), as the low estimated GI (~40), high fibre, and high protein significantly blunt postprandial glucose response; however, the deep-fried preparation increases total fat and caloric density, so portion control and preference for baked or roasted alternatives are strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation elevates total fat (18.1 g/100g) and sodium (397 mg/100g); high fibre and plant protein are cardioprotective but offset by frying oil and salt
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake of deep-fried salted broad beans due to elevated total fat from frying and near-threshold sodium content; the high dietary fibre (12.3 g/100g) and plant protein (23.8 g/100g) are cardioprotective, but these benefits are best realised through boiled, steamed, or roasted broad bean preparations with no added salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; sodium at 397 mg/100g approaches tissue-swelling threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried broad beans are not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because the deep-frying process generates AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation; choose boiled, steamed, or roasted broad beans to retain the anti-inflammatory benefits of legume fibre and protein without the AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-fried broad bean, salted (HPB Singapore food code F09010005) is a crunchy, savoury snack made from whole broad beans (Vicia faba) that have been deep-fried in vegetable oil and seasoned with salt. The deep-frying process transforms the soft legume into a hard, crispy snack with a nutty, slightly earthy flavour and a dense, crunchy texture. It is widely consumed as a packaged snack in Singapore and across Southeast Asia and East Asia, often sold in small bags at convenience stores, supermarkets, and provision shops. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 468 kcal, 23.8 g protein, 18.1 g fat, 52.5 g carbohydrate, 12.3 g dietary fibre, 5.2 g sugar, and 397 mg sodium. The high protein and exceptional dietary fibre content reflect the nutritional density of broad beans, while the elevated fat and sodium are attributable to the deep-frying and salting process. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 40 (low), moderated by the high fibre and protein. Clinically, the sodium content (397 mg/100g) is near the borderline threshold for hypertension concern, and the deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that are contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis. For individuals with type 2 diabetes, small portions are acceptable given the low GI and high fibre, but portion control is essential due to the high carbohydrate density. The food is plant-based, vegan, and gluten-free. It is classified as a processed legume snack and is most commonly consumed as a between-meal snack or appetiser.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat salted broad bean snacks?
How much sodium is in deep-fried broad beans?
Is deep-fried fava bean snack good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of broad bean snacks?
Calories in deep-fried broad beans per 100g
Can I eat broad bean snacks if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F09010005), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from published University of Sydney / Diabetes Care broad bean GI data and documented as analogous estimate.
notes: Saturated fat (2.6 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 16052 (broad beans, mature seeds, raw) adjusted for deep-frying in vegetable oil, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried salted broad bean preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F09010005) or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate based on published broad bean GI data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for deep-fried salted broad beans was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'wholegrain' which has been removed as deep-fried broad beans are not a wholegrain product; 'high_protein', 'high_fibre', and 'plant_based' are retained as accurate. No core macro fields are zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
